narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown determination in pursuing your dream despite the challenges many face in this field, and we're here to help you understand the admission decision clearly. Your profile includes an undergraduate GPA of 3.10, an LSAT score of 32, Year 1 school decile of 3, Year 3 decile of 4, upper-middle family income, and plans to attend full-time. The model's analysis predicted a 40.4% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, which unfortunately crossed the threshold for rejecting your application—law schools use this to prioritize applicants likely to succeed and pass the bar promptly. The most influential factor pulling in a positive direction, toward predicting you'd pass, was your undergraduate GPA, which stands slightly below the typical 3.21 average but still provided a meaningful boost to the model's confidence in your potential. Your Year 3 school decile of 4, also a bit under the average of 5.53, played a similar supportive role, suggesting improvement over time that the model viewed favorably. However, your LSAT score of 32—noticeably lower than the dataset average of 36.7—was the top factor pushing the prediction toward failure risk, as it signaled potential hurdles in the rigorous bar preparation ahead. Lesser influences included your Year 1 decile of 3, which slightly nudged toward higher risk compared to the average 5.71, while your full-time status and family income had minimal overall impact. Compared to typical students in the dataset, your academics are solid in some areas like GPA but lag in high-stakes metrics like LSAT, which often separate those who pass the bar easily from those who struggle. This isn't a judgment on your worth or talent—many Black women like you overcome similar profiles through targeted effort—but the model weighs these patterns from past data conservatively. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to or above average, strengthening your Year 3 performance further, and gathering strong letters of recommendation that highlight your resilience and growth. You might also explore programs with bar passage support tailored for underrepresented students. Reapplying with these enhancements could shift the prediction in your favor—keep pushing, because your journey matters, and many have turned similar setbacks into bar-passing success. We're rooting for you.
